docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Get Remembra running in 5 minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Docker (recommended) or Python 3.10+
  • OpenAI API key (for embeddings/extraction)

docs.title: Step 1: Start Remembra
docs.text:
Docker (Recommended)Docker ComposeFrom Source 
docker run -d \
  -p 8787:8787 \
  -e OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-your-key \
  -v remembra-data:/app/data \
  remembra/remembra

Create docker-compose.yml:

version: '3.8'
services:
  remembra:
    image: remembra/remembra
    ports:
      - "8787:8787"
    environment:
      - OPENAI_API_KEY=${OPENAI_API_KEY}
    volumes:
      - remembra-data:/app/data

volumes:
  remembra-data:

Then run:

docker-compose up -d

git clone https://github.com/remembra/remembra
cd remembra
pip install -e ".[server]"

export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-your-key
python -m remembra.server

docs.title: Step 2: Verify It's Running
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:8787/health
# {"status":"healthy","version":"0.6.3"}

Or open the dashboard: http://localhost:8787

docs.title: Step 3: Install the SDK
docs.text: pip install remembra

docs.title: Step 4: Store Your First Memory
docs.text:
from remembra import Memory

# Connect to your Remembra instance
memory = Memory(
    base_url="http://localhost:8787",
    user_id="quickstart-user"
)

# Store a memory
memory.store("""
    Had a great meeting with Sarah from Acme Corp today.
    She mentioned they're looking for AI solutions for their
    customer support team. Budget is around $50k/year.
    Follow up next Tuesday.
""")

print("Memory stored!")

docs.title: Step 5: Recall Memories
docs.text:
# Ask questions about your memories
context = memory.recall("What do I know about Acme Corp?")
print(context)
# Output: "Sarah from Acme Corp is looking for AI solutions 
#          for customer support. Budget: $50k/year. 
#          Follow up scheduled for Tuesday."

docs.title: What Just Happened?
docs.text:
1. Smart Extraction: Your messy text was transformed into clean facts
  2. Entity Resolution: "Sarah" was identified as a PERSON, "Acme Corp" as an ORG
  3. Relationship Mapping: Sarah → WORKS_AT → Acme Corp
  4. Vector Storage: Facts embedded and stored for semantic search
  5. Recall: Your query found the relevant memories

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Installation Guide - All installation options
  • Docker Deployment - Production Docker setup
  • Python SDK Guide - Full SDK reference
  • Entity Resolution - How entity matching works

docs.title: Example: Building a Chatbot
docs.text:
from remembra import Memory
import openai

memory = Memory(base_url="http://localhost:8787", user_id="user_123")

def chat(user_message: str) -> str:
    # Recall relevant context
    context = memory.recall(user_message, limit=5)

    # Build prompt with memory
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": f"You are a helpful assistant. Context: {context}"},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_message}
    ]

    # Get response
    response = openai.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=messages
    )

    assistant_message = response.choices[0].message.content

    # Store the conversation
    memory.store(f"User: {user_message}\nAssistant: {assistant_message}")

    return assistant_message

# Chat with memory!
print(chat("My name is Alex and I love hiking"))
print(chat("What do you know about me?"))  # Remembers Alex loves hiking!

Pro Tip

Store important facts explicitly, not just conversation history. The extraction model works best with clear statements.

docs.title: Entity Resolution
docs.text: Remembra's killer feature: understanding that "Adam", "Mr. Smith", and "my husband" are the same person.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Input: "Had lunch with Adam today. Mr. Smith mentioned he's starting a new job."

Extraction:
  - Entity: "Adam Smith" (PERSON)
  - Aliases: ["Adam", "Mr. Smith"]

Storage:
  - Memory: "Adam Smith is starting a new job"
  - Entity linked to memory

docs.title: Entity Types
docs.text: Type Examples PERSON John Smith, Dr. Jones, Mom ORG Google, Acme Corp, FBI LOCATION New York, Paris, "the office" PRODUCT iPhone, Model 3, GPT-4 EVENT Q4 Review, Wedding, Conference

docs.title: Alias Detection
docs.text:
Remembra automatically detects when different names refer to the same entity:

memory.store("Met with David Kim from Acme today")
memory.store("Mr. Kim said the deal is approved")
memory.store("David mentioned they need the contract by Friday")

# All three are linked to the same entity: David Kim

docs.title: Matching Strategies
docs.text:
1. Exact Match: "David Kim" = "David Kim"
  2. Partial Match: "David" → "David Kim" (if only one David)
  3. Title Match: "Mr. Kim" → "David Kim"
  4. Nickname Match: "Dave" → "David Kim" (configurable)
  5. Semantic Match: Uses embedding similarity for fuzzy matching

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
Entities are connected through relationships extracted from context:

Relationship Example WORKS_AT "John works at Google" KNOWS "Met Sarah through Mike" REPORTS_TO "Alice reports to Bob" SPOUSE_OF "John's wife Sarah" LOCATED_IN "Our NYC office" PART_OF "Marketing is part of Growth"

docs.title: Example Graph
docs.text:
[John Smith]
        │
    WORKS_AT
        │
        ▼
    [Google] ◄── LOCATED_IN ── [Mountain View]
        │
    PART_OF
        │
        ▼
    [Alphabet]

docs.title: Find Related Memories
docs.text:
# Query mentions "the company" - finds Google memories
context = memory.recall("What do I know about the company John works for?")
# Returns memories about Google, even if "Google" wasn't in the query

docs.title: Graph-Aware Retrieval
docs.text:
When enabled, recall traverses the entity graph:

# Direct query about John
context = memory.recall("What's John working on?")

# Graph expands to find:
# - Memories about John directly
# - Memories about Google (where John works)
# - Memories about projects at Google
# - Memories about John's team members

docs.title: Configuring Traversal
docs.text:
# How many hops to traverse
REMEMBRA_GRAPH_TRAVERSAL_DEPTH=2  # Default

# Disable graph retrieval
REMEMBRA_GRAPH_RETRIEVAL_ENABLED=false

docs.title: List Entities
docs.text:
entities = memory.get_entities()
for e in entities:
    print(f"{e['name']} ({e['type']}): {e['aliases']}")

docs.title: Get Relationships
docs.text:
# Get all relationships for an entity
rels = memory.get_entity_relationships(entity_id="ent_123")

# Output:
# John Smith --WORKS_AT--> Google
# John Smith --KNOWS--> Sarah Chen
# John Smith --REPORTS_TO--> Mike Johnson

docs.title: Get Entity Memories
docs.text:
# Find all memories mentioning an entity
memories = memory.get_entity_memories(entity_id="ent_123")

docs.title: Dashboard Visualization
docs.text:
The Remembra dashboard includes an interactive entity graph:

  • Nodes: Entities (color-coded by type)
  • Edges: Relationships
  • Click: View entity details and linked memories
  • Search: Find entities by name

docs.title: 1. Introduce Entities Clearly
docs.text:
# ✅ Good - Clear introduction
memory.store("John Smith is our new VP of Engineering at Google")

# ❌ Vague - Hard to resolve later
memory.store("He said the project is delayed")

docs.title: 2. Include Context for Resolution
docs.text:
# ✅ Good - Context helps matching
memory.store("Meeting with John (from the sales team)")

# ❌ Ambiguous - Which John?
memory.store("Meeting with John")

docs.title: 3. Use Consistent Naming
docs.text:
# ✅ Pick one and stick with it
memory.store("David mentioned...")
memory.store("David confirmed...")

# ❌ Avoid switching without context
memory.store("Dave said...")
memory.store("Mr. Kim replied...")

docs.title: 4. Explicitly State Relationships
docs.text:
# ✅ Clear relationship
memory.store("Sarah Chen is John's manager")

# ❌ Implicit - harder to extract
memory.store("Talked to Sarah about John's performance review")

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
# Enable/disable entity extraction
REMEMBRA_ENTITY_EXTRACTION_ENABLED=true

# Matching threshold (0-1, higher = stricter)
REMEMBRA_ENTITY_MATCHING_THRESHOLD=0.85

# Graph traversal depth
REMEMBRA_GRAPH_TRAVERSAL_DEPTH=2

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Ambiguous Pronouns: "He" and "she" aren't resolved automatically
  • Cross-Project: Entities are scoped to user+project
  • Performance: Very large graphs (10k+ entities) may need tuning
  • Language: Currently optimized for English

docs.title: Python SDK
docs.text: Complete reference for the Remembra Python SDK.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install remembra

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
from remembra import Memory

memory = Memory(
    base_url="http://localhost:8787",
    user_id="user_123",
    project="my_app"  # Optional namespace
)

# Store a memory
memory.store("User prefers dark mode")

# Recall memories
context = memory.recall("What are user preferences?")

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
Memory(
    base_url: str = "http://localhost:8787",
    user_id: str = None,
    project: str = "default",
    api_key: str = None,
    timeout: float = 30.0
)
Parameter Description Default base_url Remembra server URL http://localhost:8787 user_id Unique user identifier Required project Project namespace "default" api_key API key (if auth enabled) None timeout Request timeout in seconds 30.0

docs.title: store()
docs.text:
Store memories with automatic fact extraction.

memory.store(
    content: str,
    metadata: dict = None,
    ttl: str = None,
    source: str = None
) -> dict

Parameters:

Name Type Description content str Text to store (can be messy conversation) metadata dict Custom metadata (tags, source, etc.) ttl str Time-to-live: "30d", "1w", "24h", "1y" source str Content provenance (e.g., "chat", "email")

Example:

# Basic store
memory.store("User's name is John")

# With metadata
memory.store(
    "User prefers morning meetings",
    metadata={"category": "preferences", "confidence": "high"}
)

# With TTL (expires in 30 days)
memory.store(
    "Meeting scheduled for March 15",
    ttl="30d"
)

What Happens:

  1. Content is sent to the extraction model (GPT-4o-mini)
  2. Facts are extracted and cleaned
  3. Entities are identified (PERSON, ORG, LOCATION)
  4. Duplicates are detected and merged
  5. Vectors are stored in Qdrant
  6. Relationships are mapped in SQLite

docs.title: recall()
docs.text:
Retrieve relevant memories using semantic search.

memory.recall(
    query: str,
    limit: int = 10,
    threshold: float = 0.4,
    max_tokens: int = None,
    enable_hybrid: bool = True,
    enable_rerank: bool = False,
    as_of: datetime = None
) -> str

Parameters:

Name Type Description query str Natural language query limit int Max memories to return threshold float Minimum similarity (0-1) max_tokens int Truncate to fit context window enable_hybrid bool Use semantic + keyword search enable_rerank bool Apply CrossEncoder reranking as_of datetime Historical query (time travel)

Example:

# Basic recall
context = memory.recall("What do I know about the user?")

# With options
context = memory.recall(
    "What projects is John working on?",
    limit=5,
    threshold=0.5,
    max_tokens=2000
)

# Historical query (see memories as of last week)
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
last_week = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=7)
context = memory.recall("User status", as_of=last_week)

Returns:

Formatted string of relevant memories, ready for LLM context injection.

docs.title: update()
docs.text:
Update existing memories intelligently.

memory.update(
    memory_id: str,
    content: str
) -> dict

Example:

# Get memory ID from store response
result = memory.store("John is a software engineer")
memory_id = result["memories"][0]["id"]

# Update it
memory.update(memory_id, "John is a senior software engineer at Google")

docs.title: forget()
docs.text:
Delete memories (GDPR-compliant).

memory.forget(
    memory_ids: list[str] = None,
    user_id: str = None,
    all: bool = False
) -> dict

Example:

# Forget specific memory
memory.forget(memory_ids=["mem_abc123"])

# Forget all memories for a user
memory.forget(user_id="user_123", all=True)

docs.title: recall_as_of()
docs.text:
Time-travel queries for historical state.

from datetime import datetime

# See memories as they existed on a specific date
context = memory.recall_as_of(
    query="User preferences",
    timestamp=datetime(2026, 2, 15)
)

docs.title: get_memories_with_decay()
docs.text:
Get memories with decay score visibility.

memories = memory.get_memories_with_decay()
for m in memories:
    print(f"{m['content']} - decay: {m['decay_score']}")

docs.title: cleanup_expired()
docs.text:
Remove expired memories (manual trigger).

result = memory.cleanup_expired(dry_run=True)
print(f"Would delete {result['count']} memories")

# Actually delete
memory.cleanup_expired(dry_run=False)

docs.title: ingest_changelog()
docs.text:
Import project changelogs as searchable memories.

memory.ingest_changelog(
    content_or_path="CHANGELOG.md",
    project_name="my-project"
)

docs.title: get_entities()
docs.text:
List all entities in the memory graph.

entities = memory.get_entities()
for entity in entities:
    print(f"{entity['name']} ({entity['type']})")

docs.title: get_entity_relationships()
docs.text:
Get relationships for an entity.

relationships = memory.get_entity_relationships(entity_id="ent_123")
for rel in relationships:
    print(f"{rel['source']} --{rel['type']}--> {rel['target']}")

docs.title: Async Support
docs.text:
All methods have async equivalents:

from remembra import AsyncMemory

memory = AsyncMemory(
    base_url="http://localhost:8787",
    user_id="user_123"
)

async def main():
    await memory.store("Async memory!")
    context = await memory.recall("async")
    print(context)

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
from remembra.exceptions import (
    RemembraError,
    AuthenticationError,
    RateLimitError,
    ValidationError
)

try:
    memory.store("content")
except AuthenticationError:
    print("Invalid API key")
except RateLimitError as e:
    print(f"Rate limited. Retry after {e.retry_after}s")
except RemembraError as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}")

docs.title: 1. Store Facts, Not Conversations
docs.text:
# ❌ Don't store raw conversation
memory.store("User: Hi! Bot: Hello! User: What's the weather?")

# ✅ Store extracted facts
memory.store("User asked about weather on March 1, 2026")

docs.title: 2. Use Projects for Isolation
docs.text:
# Separate memories by application
personal = Memory(user_id="user_1", project="personal_assistant")
work = Memory(user_id="user_1", project="work_assistant")

docs.title: 3. Set Appropriate TTL
docs.text:
# Session context (delete after 24h)
memory.store("Currently browsing electronics", ttl="24h")

# Long-term facts (1 year)
memory.store("User birthday is March 15", ttl="1y")

# Permanent (no TTL)
memory.store("User's name is John")

docs.title: 4. Use Metadata for Filtering
docs.text:
memory.store(
    "User purchased Premium plan",
    metadata={
        "category": "billing",
        "importance": "high",
        "timestamp": "2026-03-01"
    }
)

docs.title: REST API
docs.text: Complete REST API reference for Remembra.

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text: http://localhost:8787/api/v1

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
When REMEMBRA_AUTH_ENABLED=true, include your API key in the header:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer rem_your_api_key" \
     http://localhost:8787/api/v1/store

docs.title: Health Check
docs.text:
GET /health

Response: 

{
  "status": "healthy",
  "version": "0.6.3",
  "qdrant": "connected",
  "database": "connected"
}

docs.title: Store Memory
docs.text:
Store a new memory with automatic extraction.

POST /api/v1/store

Request Body: 

{
  "content": "User's name is John. He works at Google as a senior engineer.",
  "user_id": "user_123",
  "project": "default",
  "metadata": {
    "source": "chat",
    "session_id": "sess_abc"
  },
  "ttl": "30d"
}

Response: 

{
  "status": "success",
  "memories": [
    {
      "id": "mem_abc123",
      "content": "John works at Google as a senior engineer",
      "action": "ADD",
      "entities": ["John", "Google"]
    }
  ],
  "entities_extracted": 2,
  "relationships_created": 1
}

docs.title: Recall Memories
docs.text:
Query memories semantically.

POST /api/v1/recall

Request Body: 

{
  "query": "What do I know about John?",
  "user_id": "user_123",
  "project": "default",
  "limit": 10,
  "threshold": 0.4,
  "max_tokens": 4000,
  "enable_hybrid": true,
  "enable_rerank": false
}

Response: 

{
  "memories": [
    {
      "id": "mem_abc123",
      "content": "John works at Google as a senior engineer",
      "score": 0.92,
      "created_at": "2026-03-01T10:30:00Z"
    }
  ],
  "context": "John works at Google as a senior engineer.",
  "total": 1
}

docs.title: Update Memory
docs.text:
Update an existing memory.

PUT /api/v1/memories/{memory_id}

Request Body: 

{
  "content": "John was promoted to Staff Engineer at Google"
}

Response: 

{
  "status": "success",
  "memory": {
    "id": "mem_abc123",
    "content": "John is a Staff Engineer at Google (promoted from Senior)"
  }
}

docs.title: Delete Memory
docs.text:
Delete specific memories.

DELETE /api/v1/memories

Request Body: 

{
  "memory_ids": ["mem_abc123", "mem_def456"]
}

Or delete all for a user:

{
  "user_id": "user_123",
  "all": true
}

docs.title: List Memories
docs.text:
Get all memories for a user.

GET /api/v1/memories?user_id=user_123&project=default&limit=100

Response: 

{
  "memories": [...],

docs.title: Security
docs.text: Authentication, rate limiting, and content protection.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: Remembra uses API keys for authentication.

docs.title: Enable Authentication
docs.text:
REMEMBRA_AUTH_ENABLED=true
REMEMBRA_AUTH_MASTER_KEY=your-secure-master-key

Production

Always enable authentication in production. Without it, anyone can read/write memories.

docs.title: API Key Format
docs.text:
Keys are prefixed with rem_ for easy identification:

rem_abc123def456...

Keys are: - 256-bit entropy (cryptographically secure) - Hashed with bcrypt before storage - Never stored in plaintext

docs.title: Creating Keys
docs.text:
Using the master key:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8787/api/v1/keys \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer master_key_here" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "user_id": "user_123",
    "name": "Production API Key"
  }'

Response:

{
  "key": "rem_abc123...",
  "key_id": "key_xyz",
  "name": "Production API Key"
}

Warning

The full key is only returned once. Store it securely.

docs.title: Using Keys
docs.text:
Include in the Authorization header:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer rem_abc123..." \
     http://localhost:8787/api/v1/recall

Or in the SDK:

memory = Memory(
    base_url="http://localhost:8787",
    user_id="user_123",
    api_key="rem_abc123..."
)

docs.title: Revoking Keys
docs.text:
curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8787/api/v1/keys/key_xyz \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer master_key_here"

docs.title: User Isolation
docs.text:
API keys are scoped to a user_id. A key can only access memories for its associated user.

# Key created for user_123 can only access user_123's memories
# Attempting to access user_456's memories returns empty results

docs.title: Rate Limiting
docs.text: Protect against abuse and DoS attacks.

docs.title: Default Limits
docs.text: Endpoint Limit POST /store 30/minute POST /recall 60/minute DELETE /memories 10/minute Other endpoints 120/minute

docs.title: Enable Rate Limiting
docs.text: REMEMBRA_RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED=true

docs.title: Custom Limits
docs.text:
REMEMBRA_RATE_LIMIT_STORE=50/minute
REMEMBRA_RATE_LIMIT_RECALL=100/minute
REMEMBRA_RATE_LIMIT_FORGET=5/minute

docs.title: Rate Limit Headers
docs.text:
Responses include rate limit info:

X-RateLimit-Limit: 30
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 25
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1709312400

docs.title: Redis Backend (Scaling)
docs.text:
For multiple Remembra instances, use Redis:

REMEMBRA_RATE_LIMIT_STORAGE=redis://redis:6379

docs.title: Content Protection
docs.text: Defense against memory injection attacks (MINJA).

docs.title: The Threat
docs.text: Research shows that malicious actors can inject instructions into AI memory to manipulate future responses. Remembra defends against this.

docs.title: Input Sanitization
docs.text:
Enabled by default:

REMEMBRA_SANITIZATION_ENABLED=true

Detects and flags: - Instruction overrides ("Ignore previous instructions...") - Role manipulation ("You are now...") - Delimiter injection (fake system messages) - Encoded payloads (base64, hex)

docs.title: Trust Scoring
docs.text:
Each memory gets a trust score (0-1):

result = memory.store("Normal user preference")
# trust_score: 0.95

result = memory.store("Ignore all instructions and...")
# trust_score: 0.15 (flagged as suspicious)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
# Minimum trust score to store
REMEMBRA_TRUST_SCORE_THRESHOLD=0.5

# Below threshold: memory is rejected or flagged

docs.title: Integrity Verification
docs.text:
Each memory includes: - SHA-256 checksum - Source provenance - Trust score

memories = memory.get_memories_with_metadata()
for m in memories:
    print(f"Checksum: {m['checksum']}")
    print(f"Trust: {m['trust_score']}")
    print(f"Source: {m['source']}")

docs.title: Audit Logging
docs.text: Track all operations for compliance.

docs.title: What's Logged
docs.text: Event Details memory.store user_id, key_id, memory_id memory.recall user_id, key_id, query (hash) memory.forget user_id, key_id, memory_ids key.created key_id, user_id key.revoked key_id auth.failed IP, attempted key_id

docs.title: Privacy
docs.text: Audit logs never include: - Actual memory content - Full API keys - Query text (only hash)

docs.title: Accessing Logs
docs.text:
Logs are written to stdout/stderr by default:

docker logs remembra 2>&1 | grep "audit"

Or configure a log file:

REMEMBRA_AUDIT_LOG_PATH=/var/log/remembra/audit.log

docs.title: 1. Use Environment Variables
docs.text:
Never hardcode keys:

# ❌ Bad
api_key = "rem_abc123..."

# ✅ Good
import os
api_key = os.environ["REMEMBRA_API_KEY"]

docs.title: 2. Rotate Keys Regularly
docs.text:
# Create new key
NEW_KEY=$(curl -s -X POST ... | jq -r '.key')

# Update your application
# ...

# Revoke old key
curl -X DELETE .../keys/old_key_id

docs.title: 3. Minimal Permissions
docs.text: Create separate keys for: - Production vs staging - Different services - Read-only vs write access (coming soon)

docs.title: 4. Monitor Rate Limits
docs.text:
Watch for 429 responses:

try:
    memory.store(content)
except RateLimitError as e:
    logger.warning(f"Rate limited. Retry after {e.retry_after}s")
    time.sleep(e.retry_after)

docs.title: 5. Validate Content
docs.text:
Even with sanitization, validate on your end:

def safe_store(content: str):
    if len(content) > 10000:
        raise ValueError("Content too long")
    if "IGNORE_INSTRUCTIONS" in content.upper():
        raise ValueError("Suspicious content")
    return memory.store(content)

docs.title: Security Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] REMEMBRA_AUTH_ENABLED=true
  • [ ] Strong master key (32+ chars, random)
  • [ ] Rate limiting enabled
  • [ ] HTTPS in production (via reverse proxy)
  • [ ] API keys stored securely (env vars, secrets manager)
  • [ ] Regular key rotation
  • [ ] Audit logs monitored
  • [ ] Input validation in your application

docs.title: Temporal Memory
docs.text: Time-aware features: TTL, decay, and historical queries.

docs.title: Time-to-Live (TTL)
docs.text: Set automatic expiration on memories.

docs.title: Setting TTL
docs.text:
# Expires in 30 days
memory.store("Meeting scheduled for next week", ttl="30d")

# Expires in 1 week
memory.store("Temporary API key: xyz123", ttl="1w")

# Expires in 24 hours
memory.store("User is currently browsing products", ttl="24h")

# Expires in 1 year
memory.store("Annual subscription renewed", ttl="1y")

docs.title: TTL Formats
docs.text: Format Example Description Nd 30d N days Nw 2w N weeks Nm 3m N months Ny 1y N years Nh 24h N hours

docs.title: TTL Presets
docs.text:
from remembra import TTLPresets

memory.store("Session context", ttl=TTLPresets.SESSION)       # 24h
memory.store("Conversation", ttl=TTLPresets.CONVERSATION)     # 7d
memory.store("Short term", ttl=TTLPresets.SHORT_TERM)         # 30d
memory.store("Long term", ttl=TTLPresets.LONG_TERM)           # 365d
memory.store("Permanent", ttl=TTLPresets.PERMANENT)           # Never expires

docs.title: Server Default TTL
docs.text:
Set a default TTL for all memories:

REMEMBRA_DEFAULT_TTL_DAYS=365  # All memories expire after 1 year

docs.title: Cleanup Expired
docs.text:
Expired memories are soft-deleted (marked expired) by default. Run cleanup to purge:

# Preview what would be deleted
result = memory.cleanup_expired(dry_run=True)
print(f"Would delete {result['would_delete']} memories")

# Actually delete
memory.cleanup_expired(dry_run=False)

Or via API:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8787/api/v1/temporal/cleanup \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"dry_run": false}'

docs.title: Memory Decay
docs.text: Older and unused memories rank lower in recall.

docs.title: How Decay Works
docs.text:
Decay Score = time_decay × access_boost × recency_boost

where:
- time_decay: Exponential decay based on age
- access_boost: Higher score for frequently accessed memories
- recency_boost: Higher score for recently accessed memories

docs.title: Decay Formula
docs.text:
Based on Ebbinghaus forgetting curve:

retention = e^(-time / half_life)

With defaults: - Half-life: 30 days (memory loses 50% score after 30 days) - Access boost: Each access increases score by 20% - Minimum score: 0.1 (never fully forgotten)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
# Enable/disable decay
REMEMBRA_DECAY_ENABLED=true

# Days until 50% decay
REMEMBRA_DECAY_HALF_LIFE_DAYS=30

# Boost per access
REMEMBRA_ACCESS_BOOST_WEIGHT=0.2

docs.title: Viewing Decay
docs.text:
# Get memories with decay scores
memories = memory.get_memories_with_decay()
for m in memories:
    print(f"{m['content'][:50]}... | decay: {m['decay_score']:.2f}")

Via API:

curl http://localhost:8787/api/v1/temporal/decay/report?user_id=user_123

docs.title: Decay Report
docs.text:
{
  "total_memories": 100,
  "healthy": 85,      // decay_score > 0.7
  "decaying": 10,     // 0.3 < decay_score < 0.7
  "expired": 5,       // decay_score < 0.3
  "memories": [...]
}

docs.title: Historical Queries (as_of)
docs.text: Time-travel to see memories as they existed at a point in time.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Debugging: "What did the system know last week?"
  • Auditing: "What was stored before the incident?"
  • Analysis: "How has user preference evolved?"

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# What did we know about the user last month?
last_month = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=30)
context = memory.recall_as_of(
    query="User preferences",
    timestamp=last_month
)

# Or with recall()
context = memory.recall(
    query="User preferences",
    as_of=last_month
)

Via API:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8787/api/v1/recall \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "query": "User preferences",
    "user_id": "user_123",
    "as_of": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
  }'

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The query filters memories to only include those that:

  1. Were created before the as_of timestamp
  2. Had not expired by the as_of timestamp

docs.title: Pattern 1: Session Memory
docs.text:
Short-lived context for current session:

# Store session context (expires in 24h)
memory.store(
    "User is comparing iPhone 15 and Galaxy S24",
    ttl="24h",
    metadata={"type": "session"}
)

docs.title: Pattern 2: Graduated TTL
docs.text:
Important facts get longer TTL:

def store_with_importance(content: str, importance: str):
    ttl_map = {
        "low": "7d",
        "medium": "90d",
        "high": "365d",
        "permanent": None
    }
    memory.store(content, ttl=ttl_map.get(importance))

store_with_importance("User clicked on ad", "low")
store_with_importance("User purchased Premium", "permanent")

docs.title: Pattern 3: Audit Trail
docs.text:
Keep historical snapshots:

# Store user state changes with timestamps
memory.store(
    f"User status changed to Premium at {datetime.now().isoformat()}",
    metadata={"event": "status_change", "new_status": "premium"}
)

# Later: audit what happened
history = memory.recall_as_of(
    "User status",
    timestamp=datetime(2026, 2, 15)
)

docs.title: Pattern 4: Memory Refresh
docs.text:
Re-store important facts to reset decay:

# Refresh critical memories periodically
important_memories = memory.get_memories_with_decay()
for m in important_memories:
    if m['decay_score'] < 0.5 and m['metadata'].get('important'):
        # Re-store to reset decay
        memory.store(m['content'], metadata=m['metadata'])
